lines.text: JET "EXPLODES" CHASING UFO NEAR UTICA, N. Y.: The gamy but sauce
phobic press tried pitting wits with its readership. They handed the unsus-
pecting reader a front page story without the facts, then (see July 3 New Yo
Times) presented the facts in a separate story buried in the back pages. Rea
They feared public reaction to saucer tragedy, viz: Capt. Mantell. But, wit
didn't pit right with Ted Bloecher and others such as Lester S. Parkes of Top
Kansas. They correlated the loose ends:
Let's review one "end" -- the front page story: A jet fighter returning from
"scramble" to investigate an "unidentified aircraft", plunged into tiny Wales
eleven miles southwest of Utica, about 12:30 P. M., July 2, 1954. The plane
crashed into an auto and two buildings killing four persons.
The Air Force in Washington announced the F-94-C Starfire had been sent u
on "an active air defense intercept mission." According to the U. P. releas
of July 3, "Air Force spokesmen said the cockpit of the plane . . . became un
bearably hot during the flight and that the pilot ordered his radar observer t
bail out . . . then bailed out himself at 7000 ft."
According to the A. P. release in New York Times July 3, "The air base (Gr
AFB) said, 'that fuel in the burning plane apparently had exploded when the
crashed.' They said it had not been determined whether an explosion occurr
while the jet was in the air."
"Later," according to A. P., "an AF spokesman in Washington said the inter
cepted plane was one that had failed to file a flight plan with C. A. A. or, one
that drifted off its announced flight path."
The U. P. and A. P. stories of disaster were essentially true, but because of
"disaster" angle they designated the pursued object as a "plane" rather than
UFO. Ted Bloecher, who did some checking, told me that his friend, Ed
Wilkenson, listened to a broadcast which described the incident, thus: "The
jet had successfully contacted the UFO but still could not identify it."
But Ted Bloecher's real find was in the small item buried in the New York T
(July 3) back pages. It reads:
"A silvery, balloon-like object floating high over the Utica area sent resident
rushing to their telephones to make inquiries of newspapers, police and radi
stations.
"The Utica Press estimated that more than 1000 calls about the object jamm
its switchboard between 6 and 10:00 P. M. It was reported sighted by reside
in a twenty-five mile radius extending from Rome on the west to Frankfort,
east of Utica.
"Col. Milton F. Summerfelt commandant of the Air Force Depot at Rome sa
the object appeared to be a plastic balloon about 40 ft. long and partially deflate
He theorized that it was making a gradual descent and said that if it still wer
in the area tomorrow morning a plane would be sent to investigate.
"A Mohawk Airlines pilot estimated the altitude of the object at about 20, 000
He said he saw a light apparently shining from it."
( Ref. Case File - 21)
